topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lemon polish and old lavender hung heavy in the air, a suffocating blanket that seemed to press down on my chest. I stood by the doorway of the living room, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white. "I just don't see how this is working out," I managed, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. My jaw was tight, a muscle twitching near my ear. Mom was beaming, her smile wide and entirely too bright, while Dad just offered a placid nod from his armchair. I felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with the room temperature. Every carefully constructed boundary I’d maintained for years felt suddenly porous, dissolved by this unexpected, unwelcome domesticity. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to regulate the sudden tremor in my hands before I walked away.

[story 2]

"So, this is it then?" I asked, my voice pitched just a little too high, a brittle edge underlying the question. I watched my sister unpack a box of ridiculously cheerful mugs, her laughter echoing too loudly in the quiet hallway. My shoulders were rigid, pulled up towards my ears as if bracing for a blow. It wasn't the situation itself that bothered me—it was the sheer, uninvited intrusion. I could feel the familiar tightening in my stomach, a coiled spring of resentment. "I thought we had an understanding about space," I continued, my tone carefully level, though I could feel the vibration of impatience rattling beneath the surface. I didn't look at them; instead, I focused intently on a scuff mark on the hardwood floor, meticulously tracing its outline with my eyes, refusing to meet anyone’s gaze.

[story 3]

I slammed the front door shut, the sound echoing sharply in the sudden silence of the entryway. My breath hitched, short and sharp. I leaned against the cool wood, trying to force my heart rate down, but it remained stubbornly elevated. The sheer audacity of the arrangement made my skin crawl. I could feel a profound sense of being railroaded, a complete disregard for the autonomy I’d fought so hard to establish. "This isn't how things are supposed to go," I muttered to the empty space, the words laced with a sharpness I barely recognized. I pushed off the door, moving toward the stairs with deliberate, stiff steps, each movement radiating a tightly controlled disapproval.
